headline: The Phrase 'Social Contract' Is Not in the Federal Constitution; Parties Read It Differently
context: The phrase 'social contract' does not appear in Malaysia's Federal Constitution. Different political actors invoke or contest the concept and emphasise different constitutional provisions (notably Articles 153 and 8) when discussing it.

cards.big: Major political actors invoke 'the social contract'. The phrase itself is not in the Federal Constitution, and its substance is contested.
cards.sub: Different actors emphasise different constitutional provisions when discussing the concept, producing competing readings.

cards.big: The phrase 'social contract' does not appear in the Federal Constitution. It is invoked as a political idea, not a textual provision.
cards.sub: Scholars describe it as a contested constitutional narrative anchored on Reid Commission compromises (1957) and post-independence settlements.

cards.big: UMNO framing centres on Article 153 (special position of Malays and Borneo natives) and the constitutional position of the Malay rulers.
cards.sub: DAP statements emphasise Article 8 equality while also stating it upholds Article 153. The same text supports multiple readings.

cards.big: The Reid Commission's 1957 report records constitutional compromises and recommendations on citizenship, language, religion and the special position of the Malays.
cards.sub: Quoting the Reid Report as describing 'a bargain not a contract' is not a direct textual paraphrase of the report.

cards.big: A foundational concept that different parties read differently is not a fixed text. It is a constitutional narrative that each side reads against the text.
Malaysia has had this conversation in fragments. A more honest framing is which constitutional provisions are being read together, and which are being read apart.
